back: A digraph is a set of vertices connected by directed edges where each edge has a specific direction from one vertex to another.

front: Define outdegree and indegree of a vertex in a digraph
back: Outdegree is the number of edges leaving a vertex; indegree is the number of edges entering a vertex.

front: What is a binary relation on a set?
back: A binary relation R from set A to set B is a subset of A × B (Cartesian product), defining how elements are related.
A relation R on set A is reflexive if (a,a) ∈ R for every element a ∈ A.
A relation R on set A is symmetric if (a,b) ∈ R implies (b,a) ∈ R for all a,b ∈ A.
A relation R on set A is transitive if (a,b) ∈ R and (b,c) ∈ R implies (a,c) ∈ R for all a,b,c ∈ A.
An equivalence relation is a relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
A partial order is a relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.

front: What is a strongly connected digraph?
back: A digraph is strongly connected if there is a directed path from any vertex to every other vertex.

front: Define the composition of two relations
back: If R is from A to B and S is from B to C, the composition S∘R is from A to C containing (a,c) if there exists b such that (a,b)∈R and (b,c)∈S.

front: What is the adjacency matrix of a digraph?
back: A matrix where entry a[i][j] = 1 if there is a directed edge from vertex i to vertex j, else 0.
A tournament is a digraph where for every pair of distinct vertices, exactly one directed edge exists between them.

front: Define the converse (transpose) of a digraph
back: The converse reverses the direction of all edges: if (u,v) is an edge, then (v,u) is in the converse.
A directed cycle is a closed path where edges follow the direction from one vertex back to the starting vertex.

front: What is a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)?
back: A DAG is a digraph that contains no directed cycles; useful for representing dependencies and scheduling.
